ref: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
text: Pin'ilʋgʋn la Wina'am da maal agɔl nɛ teŋ.

ref: The earth was barren, with no form of life; it was under a roaring ocean covered with darkness. But the Spirit of God was moving over the water. The First Day
text: Si'el da ka' teŋ la nii, ka zin'ig la wʋsa da an yɔɔg-yɔɔg, lik ma'aa da pɛ'ɛl, ka Wina'am Siig da laannɛ giligid ku'om la zug.

ref: God said, “I command light to shine!” And light started shining.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka li an nyain.” Ka li sid nie nyain.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka nɛɛsim la an sʋm ka o bak nɛɛsim la ka kɛ lik.

ref: and named the light “Day” and the darkness “Night.” Evening came, then morning—that was the first day. The Second Day
text: Ka Wina'am buol nɛɛsim la ye, “Nintaŋ” ka buol lik la ye, “Yʋ'ʋŋ.” Ka yʋ'ʋŋ sɔb ka bɛog nie, linɛ da an yiiga dabisir.

ref: God said, “I command a dome to separate the water above it from the water below it.”
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka si'el bɛɛ an lammiri bɛŋ ku'okanɛ bɛɛ agɔl la nɛ ku'okanɛ bɛ teŋir la.”

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Ka Wina'am buol bʋnlammir dinɛ bɛɛ agɔl la ye, “Saŋgbauŋ.” Ka yʋ'ʋŋ da sɔb ka bɛog nie dabayi' daare la.

ref: God said, “I command the water under the sky to come together in one place, so there will be dry ground.” And that's what happened.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka ku'okanɛ bɛ saŋgbauŋ la teŋir la la'as zin'ig yinne, ka kɛ ka li teŋir la nie.” Ka li sid niŋ ala.

ref: God named the dry ground “Land,” and he named the water “Ocean.” God looked at what he had done and saw that it was good.
text: Ka Wina'am buol zin'ikanɛ nie ka kʋdig la ye, “Teŋ,” ka buol ku'om la ye, “Atɛuk.” Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka li an sʋm.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to produce all kinds of plants, including fruit trees and grain.” And that's what happened.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka bʋmbʋʋda buudi wʋsa bul teŋinɛ yina. Bʋmbʋʋdbanɛ du'ad bielim nɛ tiis buudi wʋsa banɛ wan wala ka mɔr biili.” Ka li sid niŋ ala.

ref: The earth produced all kinds of vegetation. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ka teŋ la pɛ'ɛl nɛ zʋʋd nɛ tiis. Ka bʋmbʋʋda wʋsa du'a li buudi. Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka li an sʋm.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Ka yʋ'ʋŋ sɔb ka bɛog nie daba atan' daare la.

ref: God said, “I command lights to appear in the sky and to separate day from night and to show the time for seasons, special days, and years.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka bugum bɛɛ agɔl dinɛ na bak nintaŋ nɛ yʋ'ʋŋ ka an zanbin linɛ pa'an sɛɛung nɛ ʋʋn, nɛ dabisa nɛ yʋma.

ref: I command them to shine on the earth.” And that's what happened.
text: Ka kɛ ka bugum bɛ saŋgbauŋ la ni dinɛ na tis teŋgbauŋ la nɛɛsim.” Ka li sid niŋ ala.

ref: God made two powerful lights, the brighter one to rule the day and the other to rule the night. He also made the stars.
text: Wina'am da maal nɛ bugum kanda ayi'. Bugum tita'ar la da tisid nɛɛsim nintaŋ ka bugum si'asiŋ la tisid nɛɛsim yʋ'ʋŋ. O da lɛm maal nwadbibis.

ref: Then God put these lights in the sky to shine on the earth,
text: Wina'am da maali ba niŋ saŋgbana la ni ye ba tis teŋgbauŋ la nɛɛsim

ref: to rule day and night, and to separate light from darkness. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: ka sʋ'oe nintaŋ nɛ yʋ'ʋŋ ka wɛlig nɛɛsim yi likin. Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka li an sʋm.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fourth day. The Fifth Day
text: Ka yʋ'ʋŋ da sɔb ka bɛog nie daba anaasi daare la.

ref: God said, “I command the ocean to be full of living creatures, and I command birds to fly above the earth.”
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka ku'om la mɔr zimi nɛ bʋnvʋya banɛ bɛ ku'omin wʋsa, ka kɛ ka niis buudi wʋsa ian'ad agɔla.”

ref: So God made the giant sea monsters and all the living creatures that swim in the ocean. He also made every kind of bird. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ka Wina'am maal bʋnvʋbɛda banɛ bɛɛ atɛukʋn la, nɛ zimi nɛ ba buudi nɛ niis nɛ ba buudi. Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka li an sʋm.

ref: Then he gave the living creatures his blessing—he told the ocean creatures to increase and live everywhere in the ocean and the birds to increase everywhere on earth.
text: Ka Wina'am niŋi ba zug bareka ka yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka zimi la wiaki pɛ'ɛl atɛuk la ka kɛ ka niis la mɛ wiaki pɛ'ɛl teŋgbauŋ la.”

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fifth day. The Sixth Day
text: Ka yʋ'ʋŋ da sɔb ka bɛog nie daba anu daare la.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to give life to all kinds of tame animals, wild animals, and reptiles.” And that's what happened.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka teŋgbauŋ la mɔr bʋnvʋya nɛ li buudi wʋsa, yin bʋnkɔnbid nɛ bʋnvʋya banɛ vuud teŋin nɛ sian'arin bʋnkɔnbid wʋsa nɛ ba buudi.” Ka li da niŋ ala.

ref: God made every one of them. Then he looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Wina'am da maal sian'arin bʋnkɔnbid la nɛ ba buudi, nɛ yin bʋnkɔnbid nɛ ba buudi nɛ bʋnkɔnbid banɛ vuud teŋin la wʋsa nɛ ba buudi. Ka Wina'am nyɛ ka li an sʋm.

ref: God said, “Now we will make humans, and they will be like us. We will let them rule the fish, the birds, and all other living creatures.”
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl ye, “Kɛl ka ti maal ninsaal nwɛnɛ ti mɛŋi an si'em la, ka kɛ ka o sʋ'oe zimi linɛ bɛ ku'omin la, nɛ niis banɛ ian'ada agɔla nɛ bʋnkɔnbid banɛ bɛ teŋgbauŋʋn la, hali nɛ bʋnvʋya banɛ vuud teŋin la wʋsa.”

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ala ka Wina'am da maal ninsaal nwɛnɛ o mɛŋi an si'em la. Dau nɛ pu'a ka o da maal.

ref: God gave them his blessing and said: Have a lot of children! Fill the earth with people and bring it under your control. Rule over the fish in the ocean, the birds in the sky, and every animal on the earth.
text: Ka Wina'am niŋi ba zug bareka ka yɛl ye, “Du'am nɔbigi pɛ'ɛli teŋgbauŋ la, ka sʋ'oe teŋ la, nɛ zimi banɛ bɛɛ atɛukʋn, nɛ niis banɛ bɛɛ agɔl, nɛ bʋnvʋya banɛ wʋsa ken teŋin la.”

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: M tis bʋnkɔnbid banɛ bɛ teŋin la nɛ niis banɛ bɛɛ agɔl nɛ bʋnvʋya banɛ ken teŋin hali nɛ linɛ wʋsa mɔr vʋm nɛ mɔma'asa ye li aan ba diib.” Ka li da niŋ ala.

ref: God looked at what he had done. All of it was very good! Evening came, then morning—that was the sixth day.
text: Wina'am da nyɛ on maal si'el la wʋsa ka li an sʋm. Ka yʋ'ʋŋ sɔb ka bɛog nie daba ayuobʋ daare la.

ref: So the heavens and the earth and everything else were created. The Seventh Day
text: Ala ka Wina'am da maal agɔl nɛ teŋ, nɛ bʋn linɛ bɛɛ ani la wʋsa naae.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Ka daba ayɔpɔi la daar o da naae o tʋʋma la wʋsa ka vʋ'ʋs daakan la.

ref: God blessed the seventh day and made it special, because on that day he rested from his work.
text: Ka Wina'am niŋ daba ayɔpɔi la daar zug bareka ka kɛ ka li an kasi, bɔzugɔ on da maal agɔl nɛ teŋi naae la daakan ka o da vʋ'ʋs. Wina'am da maal dau nɛ pu'a si'em

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Agɔl nɛ teŋ maaligʋ da pin'il si'em nwa'. Zugsɔb Wina'am da maal agɔl nɛ teŋ la,

ref: no grass or plants were growing anywhere. God had not yet sent any rain, and there was no one to work the land.
text: tiis nɛ mɔɔd wʋsa da nam pʋ yi teŋ la zugɔ, bɔzugɔ Zugsɔb Wina'am da nam pʋ kɛ ka saa ni teŋ la zugɔ, ka ninsaal mɛ da nan kae na kua teŋ la.

ref: But streams came up from the ground and watered the earth.
text: Amaa ku'om da bunnɛ wʋ'ʋgid teŋ la.

ref: The Lord God took some soil from the ground and made a man. God breathed life into the man, and the man started breathing.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am gbin'e tanni ya'ab dau ka vʋ'ʋs o mɛŋ vʋ'ʋsʋm niŋ o nyɔnya ni, ka dau la lieb ninsaal ka vʋ'ʋsid.

ref: The Lord made a garden in a place called Eden, which was in the east, and he put the man there.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am maal lɔmbɔn'ɔg Eden teŋin la lɛbid ya-nya'aŋ baba, ka nɔk dau kanɛ ka o maal la niŋ anina.

ref: The Lord God filled the garden with all kinds of beautiful trees and fruit trees. Two other trees were in the middle of the garden. One of these gave life—the other gave the wisdom to know the difference between right and wrong.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am kɛ ka tiis nɛ ba buudi yii nɔbigi pɛ'ɛl lɔmbɔn'ɔg la. Tiis la da vɛnl ka an sʋ'ʋŋa nɛ diib. Ka nyɔvʋr tiig nɛ tikanɛ kɛt ka nid baŋid dinɛ an sʋm nɛ bɛ'ɛdi bakid da zi'e lɔmbɔn'ɔg la teŋsʋk.

ref: From Eden a river flowed out to water the garden, then it divided into four rivers.
text: Ka Kɔldatita'ar naan Eden pʋʋgin zɔt ka li ku'om dɔn'ɔsid lɔmbɔn'ɔg la. Li da pʋdignɛ zin'isa anaasi.

ref: The first one is the Pishon River that flows through the land of Havilah,
text: Yiiga kɔldaug la anɛ Pison. Kɔldaug kan pʋbidnɛ giligid Havila teŋ la ka sanlima bɛɛ ani.

ref: The second is the Gihon River that winds through Ethiopia.
text: Kɔldaug linɛ paas ayi' la anɛ Gihon. Lin da pʋbidnɛ giligid Kus teŋ la.

ref: The Tigris River that flows east of Assyria is the third, and the fourth is the Euphrates River.
text: Linɛ paas atan' la anɛ Tigiris ka mɛ pʋbidi dɔl Asuor teŋ nya'aŋ. Ka linɛ paas anaasi an Efiritis.

ref: The Lord God put the man in the Garden of Eden to take care of it and to look after it.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am nɔk dau la niŋ Eden lɔmbɔn'ɔg la ni ye o tʋm ka gɔsidi li.

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am bas dau nɔɔr ye, “Fʋ tun'e di tiis wala banɛ bɛ lɔmbɔn'ɔgin la wʋsa,

ref: except the one that has the power to let you know the difference between right and wrong. If you eat any fruit from that tree, you will die before the day is over!”
text: amaa mid ka fʋ di tikanɛ kɛt ka fʋ baŋid linɛ an sʋm nɛ bɛ'ɛd la! Fʋ ya'a dii li fʋ na kpi.”

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am yɛl ye, “Li ka' sʋ'ʋm ye dau bɛɛ o kɔn'ɔɔ. M na maal sʋŋid onɛ zemis nɛ o tis o.”

ref: So the Lord God made him fall into a deep sleep, and he took out one of the man's ribs. Then after closing the man's side,
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am kɛ ka dau la gbis, ka o yis dau la sapirig ka maali lɛbis on yis lɔsi'a la.

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,
text: Anina ka Zugsɔb Wina'am nɔk on yis sapirsi'a la maal pu'a mɔr na tis dau la.

ref: and the man exclaimed, “Here is someone like me! She is part of my body, my own flesh and bones. She came from me, a man. So I will name her Woman!”
text: Ka dau la yɛl ye, “Ɛnhee, kɔnbkaŋa anɛ mam kɔnbir ka niŋgbiŋ la mɛ an mam niŋgbiŋ. M na buol o ye pu'a, bɔzugɔ o yinɛ man dau la san'an na.”

ref: That's why a man will leave his own father and mother. He marries a woman, and the two of them become like one person.
text: Dinzug, dau na bas o ba' nɛ o ma ka la'as nɛ o pu'a ka babayi' la na lieb niŋgbiŋ yinne.

ref: Although the man and his wife were both naked, they were not ashamed.
text: Dau la nɛ o pu'a la da anɛ biel, ka mɛ pʋ zɔt nyannɛ.

ref: The snake was sneakier than any of the other wild animals that the Lord God had made. One day it came to the woman and asked, “Did God tell you not to eat fruit from any tree in the garden?”
text: Waaf la da anɛ zam sɔb hali gaad bʋnkɔnbid banɛ wʋsa ka Zugsɔb Wina'am da maal la. O da bu'os pu'a la ye, “Li an sida ye Wina'am yɛl ye ya da di tisi'a wala lɔmbɔn'ɔg nwa' nii?”

ref: The woman answered, “God said we could eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Ka pu'a la lɛbis waaf la ye, “Ti tun'e di tiwala lɔmbɔn'ɔg nwa' ni,

ref: except the one in the middle. He told us not to eat fruit from that tree or even to touch it. If we do, we will die.”
text: amaa Wina'am ye ti mid ka ti di tikanɛ bɛ lɔmbɔn'ɔg la teŋsʋk la wala, ye ti pʋn da si'isi lii, ka ti ya'a niŋ ala ti na kpi.”

ref: “God understands what will happen on the day you eat fruit from that tree. You will see what you have done, and you will know the difference between right and wrong, just as God does.”
text: Wina'am mi' ye ya ya'a di wala la ya nini na nie ka ya nwɛnɛ on Wina'am nɛ, ka mi' sʋm nɛ bɛ'ɛdi bakid.”

ref: The woman stared at the fruit. It looked beautiful and tasty. She wanted the wisdom that it would give her, and she ate some of the fruit. Her husband was there with her, so she gave some to him, and he ate it too.
text: Ka pu'a la da nyɛ ka tiig la wala an sʋm, nar nɛ diib ka lɛn vɛnli o nini ni, ka an linɛ na tis o baŋir la, o da pun'oe di ka zaŋ sieba tis o sid ka o mɛ di.

ref: At once they saw what they had done, and they realized they were naked. Then they sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves.
text: Anina ka ba nini nie ka ba gɔsi nyɛ ka ba zi'e biel, ka ba ans kekaŋ vaandi maali sɔɔn lusi ba nyan.

ref: Late in the afternoon, when the breeze began to blow, the man and woman heard the Lord God walking in the garden. So they hid behind some trees. Sin Brings a Curse
text: Zaam da ma'ae ka ba wʋm Zugsɔb Wina'am dammir lɔmbɔn'ɔgin la, ba da zɔɔ keŋ su'a tiis gbina ni.

ref: The Lord God called out to the man and asked, “Where are you?”
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am buol dau la, ka ye, “Fʋ bɛ yaanɛ?”

ref: The man answered, “I was naked, and when I heard you walking through the garden, I was frightened and hid!”
text: Ka o lɛbis ye, “M wʋmi fʋ dammir ka dabiem kpɛn'ɛm bɔzugɔ m pa'a anɛ biel, lin ka m su'a m mɛŋ.”

ref: “How did you know you were naked?” God asked. “Did you eat any fruit from that tree in the middle of the garden?”
text: Ka Wina'am lɛbis o ye, “Anɔ'ɔn yɛlif ye fʋ anɛ biellɛ? Fʋ maal bɔ? Fʋ zan'asi m nɔɔr nɛ ka di tikanɛ wala ka m daa kpa'asidif ye mid ka fʋ dii li la nɛ bɛɛ?”

ref: “It was the woman you put here with me,” the man said. “She gave me some of the fruit, and I ate it.”
text: Ka dau la lɛbis ye, “Pu'a kan ka fʋ tisim la, onɛ tisim tiig la wal ka m di.”

ref: The Lord God then asked the woman, “What have you done?” “The snake tricked me,” she answered, “and I ate some of that fruit.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am yɛl pu'a la ye, “Bɔɔ kɛ ka fʋ maal ala?” Ka pu'a la ye, “Waaf la pan'asim ka m di.”

ref: So the Lord God said to the snake: “Because of what you have done, you will be the only animal to suffer this curse— For as long as you live, you will crawl on your stomach and eat dirt.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am yɛl waaf la ye, “Fʋn maal nwa' la zug, kaanb bɛ'ɛd na bɛɛ fʋ zug bʋnkɔnbid buudi wʋsa sʋʋgin! Fʋ na vuudi fʋ pʋʋr zug, ka dit titan, hali ti paae fʋ nyɔvʋr bɛn.

ref: You and this woman will hate each other; your descendants and hers will always be enemies. One of hers will strike you on the head, and you will strike him on the heel.”
text: Ka dataadʋg na bɛ fʋn nɛ pu'a la sʋʋgin, ka lɛm bɛ fʋ biis nɛ pu'a la biis sʋʋgin. Ka o na mɛdigi fʋ zug ka fʋn mɛ na dum o nɔbkpansinkpan'ar.”

ref: Then the Lord God said to the woman, “You will suffer terribly when you give birth. But you will still desire your husband, and he will rule over you.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am yɛl pu'a la ye, “Fʋ ya'a nɔk pʋʋg m na kɛ ka fʋ nyɛ tɔɔg hali fʋ du'am daar. Fʋ ya'a ti ye fʋ du'a biis, li na zabid hali, la'an nɛ wala fʋ ya'amlɛog na bɛɛ fʋ sid san'an, ka o mɛ sʋ'ʋf.”

ref: The Lord said to the man, “You listened to your wife and ate the fruit I told you not to eat. And so, the ground will be under a curse because of what you did. As long as you live, you will have to struggle to grow enough food.
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl Dau la ye, “Fʋn siaki fʋ pu'a nɔɔr ka di tiwal kanɛ ka m digil nɔɔr ye fʋ da di la, kaanb bɛ'ɛd bɛ teŋ la zugʋ fʋ yɛla. Fʋ na namis hali ka paam diibi di, hali ti paae fʋ nyɔvʋr bɛn.

ref: Your food will be plants, but the ground will produce thorns and thistles.
text: Gɔn'ɔs nɛ mɔyaalis na yi teŋ la zug, ka fʋ na dit tiis wala nɛ mɔbiili.

ref: You will sweat all your life to earn a living; you were made out of soil, and you will once again turn into soil.”
text: Ɔnsir na yiif ka fʋ nyaan paam diibi di, hali ti paae fʋn na lɛb teŋin daasi'er, bɔzugɔ li anɛ anina ka fʋ yina. Fʋ anɛ titan, ka na lɛb titanin.”

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Adam da pʋd o pu'a la yʋ'ʋr ye Hawa, bɔzugɔ onɛ an saalib wʋsa ma.

ref: Then the Lord God made clothes out of animal skins for the man and his wife.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am nɔk bʋnkɔnbid gbana maal fuudi yɛɛl Adam nɛ o pu'a.

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am yɛl ye, “Ninsaal la yʋ'ʋn nwɛnɛ tinam nɛ, ka mi' sʋm nɛ bɛ'ɛdi bakid. Li pʋ sʋ'ʋm ye o pun'oe nyɔvʋr tiig la wala di ka vʋe saŋa wʋsaa.”

ref: So the Lord God sent them out of the Garden of Eden, where they would have to work the ground from which the man had been made.
text: Ka Zugsɔb Wina'am kad o yis Eden lɔmbɔn'ɔgin la, ye o keŋ kua teŋgbauŋ kanɛ ka o yi la na.

ref: Then God put winged creatures at the entrance to the garden and a flaming, flashing sword to guard the way to the life-giving tree.
text: Ka o kad dau la yis ka kɛ ka Kerubnam sieba gur Eden lɔmbɔn'ɔg la za'anɔɔr. Ka zaŋ sʋ'ʋg dinɛ dit tuon nɛ nya'aŋ ka li ian'ad pil-pil ka giligid zin'ig la wʋsa gu'ud suor kanɛ ken nyɔvʋr tiigin la.

ref: Adam and Eve had a son. Then Eve said, “I'll name him Cain because I got him with the help of the Lord.”
text: Adam da digin nɛ o pu'a Hawa ka o nɔk pʋʋgʋ du'a biribiŋ ka ba pʋd o yʋ'ʋr ye Kain. Ka Hawa ye, “Nɛ Wina'am paŋ ka m du'a dau.”

ref: Later she had another son and named him Abel. Abel became a sheep farmer, but Cain farmed the land.
text: Lin nya'aŋ ka o lɛn du'a o pitʋ Abɛl n paas. Abɛl da gʋ'ʋnnɛ kɔnbid, ka Kain mɛ an kpaad.

ref: One day, Cain gave part of his harvest to the Lord,
text: Ka saŋa ti paae ye ba maal maannɛ tis Wina'am la, Kain da nɔk o zʋʋd la sieba na tis Zugsɔb la,

ref: and Abel also gave an offering to the Lord. He killed the first-born lamb from one of his sheep and gave the Lord the best parts of it. The Lord was pleased with Abel and his offering,
text: ka Abɛl mɛ da zaŋ o kɔnbkanɛ ka ba du'a yiiga ka o nɔb sʋ'ʋŋa la na tis Zugsɔb la. Ka Zugsɔb sʋnf malis nɛ Abɛl nɛ o maan la,

ref: but not with Cain and his offering. This made Cain so angry that he could not hide his feelings.
text: amaa o sʋnf da pʋ malis nɛ Kain nɛ o maan laa. Ka Kain sʋnf duoe ka o lɔɔ o nindaa.

ref: The Lord said to Cain: What's wrong with you? Why do you look so angry?
text: Ka Zugsɔb yɛl Kain ye, “Bɔ ka fʋ sʋnf pɛligɛ? Bɔ ka fʋ lɔɔdi fʋ nindaa?

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: Fʋ ya'a tʋmin dinɛ an sʋm, m naan kʋ di'enɛ fʋ maan laa? Amaa fʋn pʋ tʋm linɛ an sʋm la, tʋʋmbɛ'ɛd bɛɛ fʋ za'anɔɔrini bɔɔd ye o nyaŋif, amaa mɔɔm ka nyaŋ o.”

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.
text: Ka Kain ti yɛl o pitʋ Abɛl ye, “Kɛl ka ti yii keŋ mɔɔgin.” Ka ban paae mɔɔgin la ka Kain kpɛn nwɛ' o pitʋ Abɛl n kʋʋ o.

ref: Afterwards the Lord asked Cain, “Where is Abel?” “How should I know?” he answered. “Am I supposed to look after my brother?”
text: Ka Zugsɔb la bu'os Kain ye, “Fʋ pitʋ la bɛ ya?” Ka o lɛbis ye, “Man zi'. Manɛ guri m pitʋ laa?”

ref: Then the Lord said: Why have you done this terrible thing? You killed your own brother, and his blood flowed onto the ground. Now his blood is calling out for me to punish you.
text: Ka Zugsɔb la yɛl ye, “Bɔɔ nwa' ka fʋ tʋm? Kɛlisim, fʋ pitʋ la ziim kaasid teŋin paadi m san'an na!

ref: And so, I'll put you under a curse. Because you killed Abel and made his blood run out on the ground, you will never be able to farm it again.
text: Nannanna nɔpɔɔr bɛɛ fʋ zugin, ka teŋgbauŋ la mɛ na zan'asif bɔzugɔ o ya'ae o nɔɔri di'e fʋ pitʋ la ziim yii fʋ nu'usin.

ref: When you try to farm the land, it won't produce anything for you. From now on, you'll be without a home, and you'll spend the rest of your life wandering from place to place.
text: Fʋ ya'a kua, teŋ la kʋ lɛn maal zuudi tisif ya'asɛ. Fʋ na anɛ onɛ gɔɔndi giligid dunia la zug.”

ref: “This punishment is too hard!” Cain said.
text: Kain da yɛl Zugsɔb la ye, “Namisʋg nwa' tʋʋgi m paŋ bɛn.

ref: “You're making me leave my home and live far from you. I will have to wander about without a home, and just anyone could kill me.”
text: Zina fʋ kadimi yis teŋ la pʋʋgin ka m kʋ lɛn bɛɛ fʋ tuonnɛ. M na anɛ onɛ gɔɔndi giligid dunia la zug, ka onɛ wʋsa nyɛ mam na kʋʋm!”

ref: “No!” the Lord answered. “Anyone who kills you will be punished seven times worse than I am punishing you.” So the Lord put a mark on Cain to warn everyone not to kill him.
text: Amaa ka Zugsɔb la yɛl o ye, “Li ka' ala, sɔ' ya'a kʋ Kain o na nyɛ namisʋg nɔɔra ayɔpɔi.” Ka Zugsɔb la zanbil Kain ye sɔ' ya'a nyɛɛ o, on da kʋʋ o.

ref: But Cain had to go far from the Lord and live in the Land of Wandering, which is east of Eden. More and More People
text: Kain da yi Zugsɔb la san'an keŋ bɛ Nod teŋin, Eden nya'aŋ sa.

ref: Later, Cain and his wife had a son named Enoch. At the time Cain was building a town, and so he named it Enoch after his son.
text: Kain da digin nɛ o pu'a ka o gban'e pʋʋgʋ du'a Enok. O da mɛɛdnɛ tempʋʋg, ka nɔk o biig Enok yʋ'ʋri pʋd teŋ la.

ref: Then Enoch had a son named Irad, who had a son named Mehujael, who had a son named Methushael, who had a son named Lamech.
text: Enok da du'a Irad, ka Irad du'a Mehuyael, ka Mehuyael du'a Metusael, ka Metusael du'a Lamek.

ref: Lamech and Adah had two sons, Jabal and Jubal. Their son Jabal was the first to live in tents and raise sheep and goats. Jubal was the first to play harps and flutes.
text: Ka ba da lɛn du'a o pitʋ ka on yʋ'ʋr da buon Jubal. Ka on nɛ o nya'aŋ dim wʋsa da an banɛ nwɛ'ɛd bʋnnwɛ'ɛda ka piebid wiis.

ref: Lamech and Zillah had a son named Tubal Cain who made tools out of bronze and iron. They also had a daughter, whose name was Naamah.
text: Ka Lamek pu'abil Zilla mɛ da du'a biig, ka o yʋ'ʋr buon ye Tuebal Kain. Ka onɛ mɛ da pin'il kʋnt tʋʋma wʋsa, ka o taʋn yʋ'ʋr da buon Naama.

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Ba ya'a kʋ nidiba ayɔpɔi yɔ Kain kʋʋb sam, ba na kʋ nidib pisyɔpɔi nɛ ayɔpɔi yɔ man Lamek kʋʋb sam!”

ref: Adam and his wife had another son. They named him Seth, because they said, “God has given us a son to take the place of Abel, who was killed by his brother Cain.”
text: Ka Adam nɛ Hawa da lɛn du'a biig, ka Hawa da pʋd o yʋ'ʋr ye Set, ka yɛl ye, “Wina'am tisim biig nwa' ka o lɛdig Abɛl zin'igin, Kain n kʋ Abɛl la zug.”

ref: Then the Lord said, “I won't let my life-giving breath remain in anyone forever. No one will live for more than
text: Ka Zugsɔb yɛl ye, “M vʋm la kʋ lɛm bɛ nɛ ninsaali yuug hali, bɔzugɔ o anɛ niŋgbiŋ, ka o yʋma na anɛ kɔbiga nɛ pisi ma'aa.”

ref: The children of the supernatural beings who had married these women became famous heroes and warriors. They were called Nephilim and lived on the earth at that time and even later.
text: Ka Wina'am biis la da di pu'a vɛnla la ka digin nɛ ba la, ba da du'a bibanɛ da lieb gandaad ka ba da buonnɛ ba ye Nefil dim. Ba da anɛ nimbɛda banɛ mɔr paŋ, ka ba bɛ saŋkan la nɛ li nya'aŋ.

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Ka Zugsɔb la da nyɛ nidib la tʋʋmbɛ'ɛd nɛ ba pʋtɛnda an bɛ'ɛd saŋa wʋsa si'em la,

ref: He was sorry that he had made them,
text: o sʋʋnr da san'am on maal ninsaal la zug.

ref: and he said, “I'm going to destroy every person on earth! I'll even wipe out animals, birds, and reptiles. I'm sorry I ever made them.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb la yɛl ye, “M na san'am saalib ban ka m maal dunia nwa', nɛ bʋnkɔnbid nɛ niis nɛ bʋnvuuda la, bɔzugɔ m sʋʋnr san'ami ba yɛla.”

ref: But the Lord was pleased with Noah,
text: Amaa Zugsɔb sʋnf da ma'ae Noa yɛla.

ref: and this is the story about him. Noah was the only person who lived right and obeyed God.
text: Nwa' pa'an Noa nɛ o yaas yɛla: Noa da anɛ pʋpiel ka pʋ mɔr gbɛliŋɔ, ka dɔl Wina'am bɔɔdim sʋ'ʋŋa.

ref: He had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
text: O da du'a biribis atan' ka ba yʋda an Sem nɛ Ham nɛ Jafet.

ref: God knew that everyone was terribly cruel and violent.
text: ka nyɛ ka nidib la wʋsa an pɔnrʋŋ dim dunia ni, ka dɔl suobɛ'ɛdnam hali.

ref: Get some good lumber and build a boat. Put rooms in it and cover it with tar inside and out.
text: Dinzug nɔkim tikanɛ daugʋ kpi'emi maal anrintita'ar. Pʋdigim dɔɔdi li pʋʋgin ka bia' li nɛ bi'en pʋʋgin nɛ nya'aŋ.

ref: Noah did everything God told him to do.
text: Ka Noa da tʋm linɛ wʋsa ka Wina'am da pa'al o ye o tʋm la.

ref: I'm going to send a flood that will destroy everything that breathes! Nothing will be left alive.
text: M na kɛ ka saa ni ka ku'om kena pɛ'ɛl dunia n di ninsaalib, nɛ linɛ wʋsa mɔr vʋm teŋgbauŋ la zug.

ref: But I solemnly promise that you, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law will be kept safe in the boat.
text: Amaa m na maal nɔnaar nɛf ka fʋ na kpɛn' anrʋŋʋn la, fʋn nɛ fʋ pu'a nɛ fʋ biis nɛ fʋ bipu'ab.

ref: Store up enough food both for yourself and for them.
text: Fʋ na la'as diib buudi wʋsa mɔr na su'a anina ka li an diibi tis fʋn nɛ ban wʋsa.”

ref: The Lord told Noah: Take your whole family with you into the boat, because you are the only one on this earth who pleases me.
text: Ka Zugsɔb la yɛl Noa ye, “Kpɛn'ɛm anrʋŋ la ni, fʋn nɛ fʋ yidim wʋsa, bɔzugɔ m nyɛ fʋn an ninsʋŋ si'em zʋmaʋŋ kaŋa ni.

ref: Take seven pairs of every kind of animal that can be used for sacrifice and one pair of all others.
text: Nɔkim bʋnkɔnbid banɛ an nyain ka ba mɔri maan malima la ayɔpɔi-pɔi, bʋndaug nɛ bʋnnya'aŋ, nɛ bʋnkɔnbid buudbanɛ pʋ maan malima la ayi', yi' bʋndaad nɛ bʋnnya'as,

ref: Also take seven pairs of every kind of bird with you. Do this so there will always be animals and birds on the earth.
text: nɛ niis buudi wʋsa ayɔpɔi-pɔi bʋndaad nɛ bʋnnya'as linɛ na kɛ ka ba buudi kʋ kpi'ilim teŋgbauŋ nwa' zugɔ.

ref: years old when he went into the boat to escape the flood, and he did everything the Lord had told him to do. His wife, his sons, and his daughters-in-law all went inside with him.
text: ) la, li nwadis ayi' la dabpii nɛ ayɔpɔi daar, ka ku'osuoya la yɔ'ɔs, banɛ bɛ agɔl nɛ banɛ tans teŋin la wʋsa.

ref: He obeyed God and took a male and a female of each kind of animal and bird into the boat with him.
text: Bʋnkɔnbid nɛ niimis banɛ an nyain nɛ banɛ ka' nyainnɛ; ba bʋndaad nɛ bʋnnya'as, ba bʋmbɛda nɛ bʋmbibis wʋsa mɛ da kɛn Noa san'an na kpɛn' anrʋŋʋn la nwɛnɛ Wina'am yɛl o si'em la.

ref: Seven days later a flood began to cover the earth.
text: Daba ayɔpɔi la nya'aŋ ka ku'otita'ar pɛ'ɛl teŋgbauŋ la wʋsa.

ref: The water under the earth started gushing out everywhere, the sky opened like windows, and rain poured down for
text: Ka saa ni teŋgbauŋ la ni dabpisnaasi yʋ'ʋŋ nɛ nintaŋ wʋsa.

ref: On that day Noah and his wife went into the boat with their three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and their wives.
text: Ka daakan la ka Noa nɛ o pu'a nɛ o biis Sem, Ham, nɛ Jafet nɛ ba pu'ab la da la'asi kpɛn' anrʋŋ la ni.

ref: Noah took a male and a female of every living creature with him,
text: Bʋnkɔnbid banɛ wʋsa mɔr vʋm la buudi ayi', yi' kɛn Noa san'an na kpɛn' anrʋŋ la.

ref: just as God had told him to do. And when they were all in the boat, the Lord closed the door.
text: Bʋnkɔnbbanɛ da kpɛn' anrʋŋ la ni da anɛ bʋndaug nɛ bʋnnya'aŋ nwɛnɛ Wina'am da yɛl Noa si'em la. Ka Zugsɔb la yɔ' kʋlʋŋ la.

ref: Finally, the mighty flood was so deep that even the highest mountain peaks were about seven meters below the surface of the water.
text: Ku'om la da pɛ'ɛl teŋgbauŋ la wʋsa hali lim zuobanɛ dʋ la wʋsa gaad nɔbir makir pisi.

ref: Not a bird, animal, reptile, or human was left alive anywhere on earth.
text: Bʋnvʋya banɛ da bɛ teŋgbauŋ zug la wʋsa da kpiya. Niis nɛ yin bʋnkɔnbid nɛ sian'arin bʋnkɔnbid nɛ banɛ wʋsa vuud teŋ nɛ saalib wʋsa.

ref: A hundred fifty days later, the water started going down.
text: Ka ku'om la da pɛ'ɛl teŋgbauŋ la dabisa kɔbiga nɛ pisnu.

ref: God did not forget about Noah and the animals with him in the boat. So God made a wind blow, and the water started going down.
text: Wina'am da tɛnr Noa nɛ sian'arin bʋnkɔnbid nɛ yin bʋnkɔnbid banɛ da bɛ anrʋŋʋn la yɛla ka tʋm sisi'em ka li kɛ ka ku'om la kpiɛn.

ref: God stopped up the places where the water had been gushing out from under the earth. He also closed up the sky, and the rain stopped.
text: O da yɔ' ku'osuoya banɛ tans teŋin la ka yɔ' ku'osuoya agɔl ka saa la da gu'oe niib.

ref: Then on the seventeenth day of the seventh month of the year, the boat came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountains.
text: ka lin ti paae nwadis ayɔpɔi dabis pii nɛ ayɔpɔi daar la, anrʋŋ la da zuon nɛ Ararat zuor zug.

ref: The water kept going down, and the mountain tops could be seen on the first day of the tenth month.
text: Ku'om la da maligim siednɛ hali, ka nwadis piiga la pin'ilʋg daar ka zuoya zut yʋ'ʋn nied.

ref: Forty days later Noah opened a window to send out a raven, but it kept flying around until the water had dried up.
text: ka tʋm agaung ka o yina ian'adi giligid ka pʋ lɛb naa, hali nɛ ku'om la ti kpiɛn.

ref: Noah wanted to find out if the water had gone down, so he sent out a dove.
text: Ka Noa da lɛn tʋm dawan ye o gɔs ye ku'om la kpiɛn yaa?

ref: Deep water was still everywhere, and when the dove could not find a place to land, it flew back to the boat. Then Noah held out his hand and helped it back in.
text: Ka ku'om la da nan pɛ'ɛl zin'ig wʋsa la zug, dawan da pʋ paam zin'ikanɛ ka o na zuonɛ, ka gʋ'ʋŋi lɛb Noa san'an anrʋŋʋn la na. Ka Noa tiɛn o nu'ugʋ gban'e o kpɛn'ɛs.

ref: Seven days later Noah sent the dove out again.
text: Ka o da gu'us daba ayɔpɔi ka lɛn tʋm dawan la ya'as.

ref: It returned in the evening, holding in its beak a green leaf from an olive tree. Noah knew the water was finally going down.
text: Ka dawan lɛb zaam na ka mɔr olif vanma'asiri o nɔɔrin. Ani ka Noa yʋ'ʋn baŋ ye ku'om la kpiɛn naae.

ref: He waited seven more days before sending the dove out again, and this time it did not return.
text: Ka o lɛn gu'us daba ayɔpɔi ka lɛn tʋm dawan la ya'as, ka o yi ka pʋ lɛn lɛb naa.

ref: years old. And by the first day of that year, almost all the water had gone away. Noah made an opening in the roof of the boat and saw that the ground was getting dry.
text: ) la, li nwadig yinne pin'ilʋg daar ka ku'om la kpiɛn naae teŋgbauŋ la zug. Ka Noa yis dinɛ ligil anrʋŋ la ka nyɛ ka teŋ la wʋsa kʋdig.

ref: By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was completely dry.
text: Nwadis ayi' la dabpisi nɛ ayɔpɔi la daar ka teŋgbauŋ la yʋ'ʋn kʋdig wʋsa.

ref: God said to Noah,
text: Ka Wina'am yɛl Noa ye,

ref: “You, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law may now leave the boat.
text: “Yim anrʋŋʋn la, fʋn nɛ fʋ pu'a nɛ fʋ biis nɛ ba pu'ab la.

ref: Let out the birds, animals, and reptiles, so they can mate and live all over the earth.”
text: Mɔr bʋnvʋya kɔn'ɔb-kɔn'ɔb banɛ an niimis nɛ bʋnkɔnbid nɛ banɛ wʋsa vuud teŋin la yina ka ba du'a nɔbigi pɛ'ɛl dunia la.”

ref: After Noah and his family had left the boat,
text: Ka Noa yʋ'ʋn yina nɛ o biis, nɛ o pu'a, nɛ o bipu'ab la.

ref: the living creatures left in groups of their own kind. The Lord's Promise for the Earth
text: Bʋnkɔnbid la wʋsa nɛ banɛ wʋsa vuud teŋin nɛ niis nɛ linɛ wʋsa bɛ teŋgbauŋʋn la da yi anrʋŋʋn la fuor-fuor.

ref: Noah built an altar where he could offer sacrifices to the Lord. Then he offered on the altar one of each kind of animal and bird that could be used for a sacrifice.
text: Ka Noa mɛ' maan bimbimi tis Zugsɔb la, ka nɔk bʋnkɔnbid nɛ niis banɛ an nyain ka ba mɔri maan malʋŋ la maal maannɛ tis o.

ref: The smell of the burning offering pleased the Lord, and he said: Never again will I punish the earth for the sinful things its people do. All of them have evil thoughts from the time they are young, but I will never destroy everything that breathes, as I did this time.
text: Ka Zugsɔb la wʋmmi li nyuug ka li malis o, ka o yɛl o sʋʋnrin ye, “M kʋ lɛm pɔ nɔɔri tis teŋ la ninsaal zugɔ, la'an nɛ ninsaal wʋsa pʋtɛn'ɛre an bɛ'ɛd lin yi o biilimin sa laa. M mɛ kʋ lɛn san'am bʋnvʋya nwɛnɛ mam daa maal si'em laa.

ref: As long as the earth remains, there will be planting and harvest, cold and heat; winter and summer, day and night.
text: Teŋgbauŋ la ya'a nan kpɛm bɛ, bʋdib nɛ bunib saŋa, waad nɛ tʋʋlʋg saŋa, sɛɛung nɛ ʋʋn, nintaŋ nɛ yʋ'ʋŋ kʋ gu'oe.”

ref: God said to Noah and his sons: I am giving you my blessing. Have a lot of children and grandchildren, so people will live everywhere on this earth.
text: Ka Wina'am niŋ Noa nɛ o biis la bareka ka yɛli ba ye, “Du'ami ka nɔbigi pɛ'ɛli teŋgbauŋ la.

ref: All animals, birds, reptiles, and fish will be afraid of you. I have placed them under your control,
text: Bʋnkɔnbid wʋsa na zɔɔ ya dabiem, bʋnkɔnbid nɛ niis nɛ zimi nɛ banɛ vuud teŋin la wʋsa, bɔzugɔ m nɔki ba niŋi ya nu'usin,

ref: and I have given them to you for food. From now on, you may eat them, as well as the green plants that you have always eaten.
text: ye ba an diibi tisi ya. Nwɛnɛ man da tisi ya zʋʋd wʋsa ye ya dit si'em la, ala mɛn ka m tisi ya si'el wʋsa.

ref: But life is in the blood, and you must not eat any meat that still has blood in it.
text: Amaa mid ka ya ɔnbi ni'im kanɛ ka li ziim nan kpɛm bɛɛ li ni.

ref: I want you and your descendants to have many children, so people will live everywhere on earth.
text: Amaa ya'a anɛ fʋn Noa nɛ fʋ biis la, du'ami ka nɔbigi pɛ'ɛli dunia la.”

ref: Again, God said to Noah and his sons:
text: Wina'am da lɛn yɛl Noa nɛ o biis la ye,

ref: I am going to make a solemn promise to you and to everyone who will live after you.
text: “M yʋ'ʋn maal nɔnaar nɛ yanam nɛ ya yaas banɛ na dɔlli ya nya'aŋ,

ref: I promise every living creature that the earth and those living on it will never again be destroyed by a flood.
text: M maal nɔnaar la nɛ ya ye ku'om kʋ lɛn san'am bʋnvʋr wʋsa bɛɛ teŋgbauŋ ya'asɛ.”

ref: The rainbow that I have put in the sky will be my sign to you and to every living creature on earth. It will remind you that I will keep this promise forever.
text: M na maal dindɛog bɔɔnlim niŋ saŋgbauŋin ka li na an m nɔnaar la zanbinnɛ tis man nɛ ya yaas banɛ na bɛ teŋgbauŋʋn la.

ref: When I send clouds over the earth, and a rainbow appears in the sky,
text: M ya'a ti kɛ ka saŋgbana yina ka dindɛog buoɛn,

ref: Abram went as far as the sacred tree of Moreh in a place called Shechem. The Canaanites were still living in the land at that time,

text: Yɛlim ye fʋ anɛ m taʋn ka ba na'asi m ka kʋ kʋʋmi fʋ zugɔ.”

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Ka dau sɔ' yi tanpin la zɔɔ mɔr labaari kena tis Abram onɛ an Hibiru nid la. Abram yir da kpi'enɛ Mamir onɛ an Ammon nid la tititada. Mamir nɛ o pitib banɛ an Anɛr nɛ Esikol da mɔri nɔnaar nɛ Abram.

ref: that his nephew Lot had been taken away. Three hundred and eighteen of Abram's servants were fighting men, so he took them and followed the enemy as far north as the city of Dan.

text: Yʋ'ʋŋ kan la ka Abram pʋdig o dap la ka ba zab nɛ ba ka kadi ba mɔri paae Hoba dinɛ bɛ Damaskus tempʋʋg datiuŋ baba la.

ref: and Abram went after them. He brought back his nephew Lot, together with Lot's possessions and the women and everyone else who had been captured. Abram Is Blessed by Melchizedek
text: O da paam la'ad la wʋsa faaɛn ka mɔr o pitʋ biig Lot nɛ o la'ad wʋsa, nɛ pu'ab wʋsa nɛ nidib la wʋsa lɛb na. Melkizedek niŋ Abram zug albareka

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Abram n da zabi nyaŋ Kedolma na'ab nɛ na'abanɛ wʋsa da la'as nɛ o ka lɛbid na la, Sodom Na'ab da yina sia'al o Seave Bɔn'ɔg zin'igin.

ref: King Melchizedek of Salem was a priest of God Most High. He brought out some bread and wine
text: Ka Melkizedek onɛ an Salem Na'ab la mɔr diib nɛ daam yina. O da anɛ Wina'am Onɛ Gaad Si'el Wʋsa la maanmaan.

ref: and said to Abram: “I bless you in the name of God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth.

ref: All praise belongs to God Most High for helping you defeat your enemies.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
text: Ka bareka mɛ bɛ nɛ Wina'am Onɛ Gaad Si'el Wʋsa la, bɔzugɔ o nɔki fʋ dataasi niŋi fʋ nu'usin.” Ka Abram da yis la'abanɛ ka o fan' la wʋsa yinne piiga pʋʋgin tis o.

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”

ref: Afterwards, Rebekah and the young women who were to travel with her prepared to leave. Then they got on camels and left with Abraham's servant and his men.

text: Ka Isaak onɛ yiri da bɛ Negef teŋgbauŋʋn la, da yi Berlahairoi baba kena.

ref: and they had six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.

ref: Nations will be your servants and bow down to you. You will rule over your brothers, and they will kneel at your feet. Anyone who curses you will be cursed; anyone who blesses you will be blessed.”
text: Tɛɛns wʋsa nidib na tʋm tisif. Ba na igini fʋ tuonnɛ na'asif. Fʋ na an zugdaan fʋ ba'abiis wʋsa tuon ka fʋ mabiis na igini fʋ tuon. Onɛ pɔ bɛ'ɛd wʋsa tisif, bɛ'ɛd na dɔl o. Ka onɛ niŋi fʋ zug bareka, albareka na dɔl o.”

ref: Right after Isaac had given Jacob his blessing and Jacob had gone, Esau came back from hunting.
text: Isaak n da niŋ Jakob zug albareka naae ka o duoe yi la, li da pʋ yuugɛ ka o bier Esau mɛ yi tɔn'ɔsʋgʋ paae na.

ref: He cooked the tasty food, brought it to his father, and said, “Father, please sit up and eat the meat I have brought you, so you can give me your blessing.”
text: Ka mɛ maal dikanɛ malisi mɔri kena tis o saam la. Ka yɛl o ye, “M ba', duom di diib la, ka niŋim zug bareka!”

ref: “Who are you?” Isaac asked. “I am Esau, your first-born son.”
text: Ka o saam Isaak bu'os o ye, “Fʋ aan anɔ'ɔnɛ?” Ka o lɛbis ye, “M anɛ Esau fʋ bikpɛɛm la.”

ref: Isaac started trembling and said, “Then who brought me some wild meat right before you came in? I ate it and gave him a blessing that cannot be taken back.”
text: Ka Isaak kirid gba-gba-gba ka yɛl ye, “Ka anɔ'ɔnɛ la tɔn'ɔsi kena maal diib na tisim la? M di'e di ka niŋ o zug bareka ka fʋ mɛ nan kpɛn kpɛn'ɛd na la. O mɛ sid na nyɛ albareka.”

ref: Isaac answered, “Your brother tricked me and stole your blessing.”
text: Amaa o lɛbis ye, “Fʋ pitʋ la kennɛ na pan'asim ka di'e fʋ albareka la.”

ref: Esau replied, “My brother deserves the name Jacob, because he has already cheated me twice. The first time he cheated me out of my rights as the first-born son, and now he has cheated me out of my blessing.” Then Esau asked his father, “Don't you have any blessing left for me?”
text: Ka Esau yɛl ye, “Ya'a an sida, bi'esʋg kae ka ba pʋd o yʋ'ʋr ye Jakob ka li gbin an ‘Zam sɔb la!’ Nɔɔra ayi' nwa' ka o zam mam. O da faanm kpɛuŋ faar ka nannanna ka o lɛn faaɛn m albareka!” Ka o bu'os o saam la ye, “Fʋ pʋ kɛ albareka baa bi'ela digil mam mɛnɛ?”

ref: “My son,” Isaac answered, “I have made Jacob the ruler over you and your brothers, and all of you will be his servants. I have also promised him all the grain and grapes that he needs. There's nothing left that I can do for you.”
text: Ka Isaak lɛbis o ye, “M kɛnɛ ka o aan fʋ zugdaan ka kɛ ka o ba'abiis wʋsa aan o yammis, ka o na mɔr zʋʋd nɛ daam wʋsa. M biiga, bɔ ka m ye m lɛm maali tisi fɔ?”

ref: “Father,” Esau asked, “don't you have more than one blessing? You can surely give me a blessing too!” Then Esau started crying again.
text: Ka Esau lɛn yɛl o ye, “M ba', fʋn mɔri albareka yinne ma'aanɛ? Aba, kʋdim niŋim man zug bareka mɛn; m ba'!” Ka o kaas bɛdegʋ.

ref: So his father said: “Your home will be far from that fertile land, where dew comes down from the heavens.
text: Ka o saam Isaak yɛl o ye, “Fʋ bɛllim zin'ig na lal nɛ pɔɔd banɛ mɔr kʋ'ʋlʋm, ka mɛligim pʋn kʋ luu anina.

ref: You will live by the power of your sword and be your brother's slave. But when you decide to be free, you will break loose.”
text: Fʋ na mɔr sʋ'ʋgʋ zabid ka na an onɛ tʋmmi tisid Jakob. Amaa saŋa kenna ka fʋ kʋ lɛn siaki aan o yammʋgɔ ka na sʋ'oe fʋ mɛŋ.”

ref: Esau hated his brother Jacob because he had stolen the blessing that was supposed to be his. So he said to himself, “Just as soon as my father dies, I'll kill Jacob.”
text: Ka Esau kisig Jakob, ka li anɛ o saamme daa niŋ albareka si'a niŋ o la zug. O da tɛn'ɛs o sʋʋnrin sa ye, “M saam ya'a kpi ka ti maal o kʋʋri naae m na kʋʋ m pitʋ Jakob!”

ref: When Rebekah found out what Esau planned to do, she sent for Jacob and told him, “Son, your brother Esau is just waiting for a chance to kill you.

ref: Rebekah later told Isaac, “Those Hittite wives of Esau are making my life miserable! If Jacob marries a Hittite woman, I'd be better off dead.”

text: Ka Isaak buol Jakob ka kaanb, kaanb sʋŋʋ tis o ka sa'al o ye, “Da di pu'a yi Keenan buudi la nii.

ref: Isaac then sent Jacob to stay with Rebekah's brother Laban, the son of Bethuel the Aramean. Esau Marries the Daughter of Ishmael
text: Ka Isaak kɛ ka Jakob keŋ Padanaram paae Laban onɛ saamme an Betuel ka o an Aram teŋ nid, ka o taʋn an Rebɛka onɛ an Jakob nɛ Esau ma la san'an.

ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Esau da wʋm ye Isaak niŋ Jakob zug albareka ka kɛ ka o keŋ Padanaram teŋin ye o di pu'a, ka mɛ wʋm ye o yɛl o ye, “Mit ka fʋ di pu'a Keenan dimin la,”

ref: So he married Ishmael's daughter Mahalath, who was the sister of Nebaioth and the granddaughter of Abraham. Jacob's Dream at Bethel
text: Alazug, o da duoe keŋ Isimael onɛ an Abraham biig la san'an dii o bipuŋ Mahalaf onɛ taʋn da an Nebaiof la paas pu'a banɛ ka o pʋn dɛŋi di sa la. Jakob n zaansim Betel teŋin la yɛla

text: Jakob n da yi Beerseba wa'ae Haran teŋin la,

text: o da paae zin'isi'a ka winnig li, ka o zin'inɛ anina. O da nɔk kugir linɛ bɛɛ anina la yinne digil ka kʋk o zug.

ref: The Lord was standing beside the ladder and said: I am the Lord God who was worshiped by Abraham and Isaac. I will give to you and your family the land on which you are now sleeping.

ref: Your descendants will spread over the earth in all directions and will become as numerous as the specks of dust. Your family will be a blessing to all people.

text: Ani ka Jakob zi'el nɔkaŋa ye, “Wina'am ya'a bɛ nɛ m ka gurimi m ken nwa' pʋʋgin, ka tisim diib nɛ fuud,

ref: As Jacob continued on his way to the east,
text: Ka Jakob keŋ paae teŋ kanɛ bɛ ya-nya'aŋ la.

ref: While Jacob was still talking with the men, his cousin Rachel came up with her father's sheep.

ref: Leah exclaimed, “I'm happy now, and all the women will say how happy I am.” So she named him Asher. Love Flowers
text: ka Lea ye, “M sʋʋnr ma'aya. M pu'a taab na buon mam ye sumalisim sɔb!” Ka pʋd o yʋ'ʋr ye Aser.

ref: During the time of the wheat harvest, Reuben found some love flowers and took them to his mother Leah. Rachel asked Leah for some of them,
text: Ka li ti paae ki bunib saŋa ka Reuben keŋ pɔɔgin ka nyɛ du'am tiim nya'a mɔr na tis o ma Lea, ka Rakɛl yɛl o ye, “M bierɛ, nɔkimi fʋ biig du'am tiim nya'a la tisim.”

ref: but Leah said, “It's bad enough that you stole my husband! Now you want my son's love flowers too.” “All right,” Rachel answered. “Let me have the flowers, and you can sleep with Jacob tonight.”
text: Ka Lea lɛbis o ye, “Fʋn faanm sidi m san'an la pʋ siakɛ, ka fʋ lɛn ye fʋ faanm du'am tiim pɛ'ɛsɛɛ?” Ka o lɛbis ye, “Tɔ'! Fʋn ya'a na siaki tisim ti-nya'a la, mam mɛ na siak ka Jakob kpɛn' fʋ san'an yʋ'ʋŋ kaŋa nwa'.”

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Zaam da ma'aya ka Jakob yi pɔɔgine kunna ka Lea yii tʋ'ʋs o ka yɛl o ye, “Asɛɛ fʋ digin nɛ m yʋ'ʋŋa bɔzugɔ m nɔki m biig du'am ti-nya'a la tiaki m nintaa dabisir la ye fʋ kpɛn' man san'an na.” Ka o da digin nɛ o yʋ'ʋŋ kan,

ref: and God answered Leah's prayers by giving her a fifth son.
text: ka Wina'am wʋm Lea sɔsʋg ka o nɔk pʋʋgʋ du'a biribiŋ onɛ pɛ'ɛsi anu n tis Jakob.

ref: Leah shouted, “God has rewarded me for letting Jacob marry my servant,” and she named the boy Issachar.
text: Ka Lea yɛl ye, “M nɔki m tʋmtʋm pu'ak la tisim sid ka Wina'am yɔɔm linzug!” Ka o pʋd o ye Isaka.

ref: she exclaimed, “God has given me a wonderful gift, and my husband will praise me for giving him six sons.” So she named the boy Zebulun.
text: Ka Lea yɛl ye, “Wina'am tisim bʋnsʋŋ hali. Nannanna m sid na tisim girima bɔzugɔ m du'a biribis ayuobʋ tis o!” Ka pʋd o yʋ'ʋr ye Zebulun.

ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Lin nya'aŋ ka o du'a bipuŋ ka pʋd on ye Daina.

ref: Finally, God remembered Rachel—he answered her prayer by giving her a son. “God has taken away my disgrace,” she said.
text: ka o nɔk pʋʋgʋ du'a biribiŋ ka ye, “Wina'am yisim nyannin!”

ref: “I'll name the boy Joseph, and I'll pray that the Lord will give me another son.” Jacob and Laban
text: Ka o pʋd o ye Josef. Ka yɛl ye, “Wina'am paasim biribiŋ ya'as!” Jakob n kɛ ka o kɔnbid zu'oe si'em

ref: After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban, “Release me from our agreement and let me return to my own country.
text: Lin nya'aŋ ka Jakob yɛl Laban ye, “Basim ka m lɛbi kul m mɛŋ teŋin.

ref: You know how hard I've worked for you, so let me take my wives and children and leave.”
text: Nɔkimi m pu'ab nɛ m bibanɛ yɛla ka m tʋm tisif la tisim ka m kul. Fʋ mɛŋ mi' mam tʋm tʋʋm si'a tisif.”

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Ka Jakob lɛbis o ye, “Fʋ mɛŋ mi' mam tʋm tisif ka gɔsi fʋ bʋnkɔnbid sʋ'ʋŋa si'em.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Fʋn da mɔr bi'ela ka m kena ka ba yʋ'ʋn nɔbig bɛdegʋ, ka Zugsɔb niŋi fʋ zug bareka zin'is wʋsa, man paae kpɛla hali nɛ zina. Amaa bʋndaar ka m na tʋm si'eli tisim mɛŋ yidima?”

ref: “How much do you want me to pay you?” Laban asked. Then Jacob told him: I don't want you to pay me anything. Just do one thing, and I'll take care of your sheep and goats.
text: Ka o bu'os o ye, “Bɔ ka fʋ bɔɔd ye m tisi fɔ?” Ka Jakob lɛbis ye, “Da tisim si'ela, amaa fʋ ya'a na maal bʋnkaŋa ma'aa tisim, m na keŋ gɔsi fʋ kɔnbid la ka guri ba ya'as.

ref: Let me go through your flocks and herds and take the sheep and goats that are either spotted or speckled and the black lambs. That's all you need to give me.
text: Kɛl ka m keŋi fʋ kɔnbidin la zina gaans pɛpʋpɔnra la ba kɔn', nɛ pɛbil sabilis, nɛ bʋpʋpɔnra la ba kɔn', ka ba na aan m yɔɔd.

ref: In the future you can easily find out if I've been honest. Just look and see if my animals are either spotted or speckled, or if the lambs are black. If they aren't, they've been stolen from you.
text: Ka bɛog sa fʋ ya'a ti kena ye fʋ vɛɛnsi gɔs m bʋnkɔnbban ka fʋ tisim la, m na an sida sɔb; ka bʋnkɔnbʋg kanɛ ka' pʋpɔnrʋg bɛɛ sabilig la fʋ na baŋ ye m zu nɛ.”

ref: “I agree to that,” was Laban's response.
text: Ka Laban yɛl ye, “Tɔ', m siakya!”

ref: Before the end of the day, Laban had separated his spotted and speckled animals and the black lambs from the others and had put his sons in charge of them.
text: Daakan la ka o yis bʋraad nɛ bʋnya'as banɛ an pʋpɔnra la ka lɛn gɛndig pielim, nɛ pɛrasabilis la, nɔki ba tis o biis ye ba gur.

ref: Then Laban made Jacob keep the rest of the sheep and goats at a distance of three days' journey.
text: O da kɛnɛ ka o biis la mɔri ba keŋ lalli wʋʋ daba atan' ken, ka basi ba anina, ka Jakob on nan kpɛn gɔsid Laban kɔnbbanɛ kpɛlim la.

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Amaa Jakob da nwaae tiis sieba wila pigisi li pa'ad la sieba ka bas sieba ka li an pʋpɔnra-pʋpɔnra

ref: Then he put the branches where the sheep and goats would see them while they were drinking from the water trough. The goats mated there
text: ka nɔki li sɛɛl zin'ibanɛ ka kɔnbbanɛ bɔɔd dʋʋb la kenna ye ba nu ku'om ka li sɛɛ ba tuon.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Saŋkanɛ wʋsa ka kɔnbnya'aŋ sʋma ti siak dʋʋb, Jakob da ɛɛn nɔk nɛ dapʋpɔnra la sɛ, ka ba gɔsidi li ka dʋt.

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: Amaa li ya'a anɛ kɔnbnya'as banɛ tadig la siak dʋʋb o pʋ sɛɛd daad la. Ka lin kɛ ka bʋnkɔnbid banɛ tadig la an Laban din ka banɛ kpi'em la lieb on din.

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Di da niŋnɛ ala ka Jakob lieb arezak daan hali. O da mɔr kɔnbid bɛdegʋ nɛ yammis nɛ yʋgʋma nɛ bʋmis.

ref: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were complaining, “Jacob is now a rich man, and he got everything he owns from our father.”
text: Di da kena ka Jakob wʋm ye Laban biribis la yɛt ye, “Jakob zu nɛ ti saam bʋn wʋsa, o kpan'am la wʋsa da anɛ ti saam din.”

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Ka Jakob nyɛ ka Laban bɛllim zaŋi ken o san'an pʋ lɛn nwɛnɛ lin da an si'em la.

ref: One day the Lord said, “Jacob, go back to your relatives in the land of your ancestors, and I will be with you.”
text: Ka Zugsɔb yɛl Jakob ye, “Lɛmmi fʋ saam teŋin nɛ fʋ nidibin, ka m na bɛ nɛf.”

ref: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the pasture where he kept his sheep,
text: Ka Jakob tʋm buol Rakɛl nɛ Lea ye ba kem o san'an mɔɔgin zin'ikanɛ ka o bʋnkɔnbid la bɛ la.

ref: and he told them: Your father isn't as friendly with me as he used to be, but the God my ancestors worshiped has been on my side.
text: Ka o yɛli ba ye, “M nyɛ ka ya saam bɛllim zaŋi ken mam san'an pʋ lɛn nwɛnɛ lin da an si'em, amaa m saam Wina'am la bɛ nɛ mam.

ref: years to buy your sheep and goats. During that time you kept changing my wages.
text: Ya mi' man tʋm nɛ m paŋ si'em tisi ya saam la.

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: Amaa ya saam zam mam hali ka tiaki m yɔɔd nɔɔr piiga. Amaa Wina'am pʋ kɛ ka o san'am mam.

ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: O ya'a ye, ‘Pɛpʋpɔnra la na anɛ fʋ bʋn,’ ba wʋsa da du'adi pɛpʋpɔnra, ka o mɛ ya'a da ye, ‘Pɛlasalasa la na anɛ fʋ bʋn,’ ba wʋsa du'adi lasa-lasa.

ref: That's how God has taken sheep and goats from your father and given them to me.
text: Ala ka Wina'am nɔki ya saam bʋnkɔnbid la tis mam.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Kɔnbid la dʋʋb saŋa ka m da zaansim nyɛ ka bʋraad banɛ dʋt bʋnya'as la da anɛ bʋmpʋpɔnra nɛ lasa-lasa.

ref: Then God's angel called me by name. I answered,
text: Ka Wina'am maliak buolim zaansʋŋin la ye, ‘Jakob!’ Ka m siak ye, ‘Naa, manɛ nwa'!’

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Ka o ye, ‘Gɔsima, bʋraad banɛ dʋt la wʋsa anɛ pʋpɔnra, nɛ lasa-lasa. Man nyɛ Laban n maannif si'em la zug ka m niŋ ala.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Manɛ an Betel teŋin Wina'am la, zin'ikanɛ ka fʋ da nɔk kugiri ba'a zi'el ka bu'oe malʋŋ kpaam niŋ ka pɔ nɔɔri tisim la. Nannanna basim teŋ kaŋa ka lɛbi fʋ nidib teŋin.’”

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: Ayei! O pʋ gɔs ka tinam anɛ saammaa? O pʋ kuosi ti nɛ ka di'e dinɛ ka ba yɔɔ ti yɛla la dii naae?

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: Sida arezak kanɛ ka Wina'am nɔki yii ti saam san'an la anɛ tinam nɛ ti biis din. Dinzugɔ tʋm dinɛ wʋsa ka Wina'am yɛlif.”

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Ka Jakob duoe nɔk o pu'ab la nɛ o biis la ban'al yʋgʋma.

ref: northern Syria for the home of his father Isaac in Canaan. Jacob took along all his flocks, herds, and other property.
text: Ka kad bʋnkɔnbid la galis tuon, nɛ la'abanɛ ka o paam Padanaram teŋin la lɛbid Keenan, o saam Isaak san'an.

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Laban n, da gaad ye o kies o pɛ'ɛs kɔnbid la, ka Rakɛl zuu o saam Laban yir wina.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: Jakob da nɔk on mɔr si'el wʋsa n zɔɔ lɔ'ɔŋ Efiritis kɔldaug la kennɛ kpa'ad Gilead linɛ an zuoya teŋ la.

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Daba atan' daar ka ba nyaan yɛl Laban ye Jakob zɔya.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Ka o duoe la'as o ba'abiis ka ba kad Jakob bugiri keŋ daba ayɔpɔi ka paae o Gilead linɛ an zuoya teŋin la.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Ka Laban onɛ an Aram nid la yʋ'ʋn da nyɛ zaansʋŋʋn yʋ'ʋŋ ka Wina'am yɛl o ye, “Gu'usimi fʋ mɛŋ! Mid ka fʋ pian' linɛ an sʋm bɛɛ bɛ'ɛdi tis Jakob!”

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Ka Laban yʋ'ʋn keŋ paae Jakob san'an. Ka Jakob ti' o sʋga Gilead zuoya teŋin, ka Laban nɛ o nidib la mɛ paae na ti' ba sʋga anina mɛn.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Ka Laban yɛl Jakob ye, “Bɔɔ la ka fʋ tʋm? Fʋ pan'asimi mɔri m pu'ayuos la zɔ wʋʋ tanpin ka ba gban'e ba nɛ.

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Bɔ ka fʋ pan'asim ka su'a zɔ? Bɔɔ maal ka fʋ pʋ yɛlim ka m kɛ ka fʋ keŋ nɛ sumalisim, nɛ yʋʋma, ka siyada nwɛ'ɛd ka wiis piebida?

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: Fʋ pʋ kɛ ka m kaanbi tisim yaas nɛ m pu'ayuos la ka kɛɛsi ba sʋ'ʋŋa. Fʋ tʋmnɛ yalim tʋʋma!

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: M mɔr suori na tun'e san'amif, amaa yʋ'ʋŋin ka fʋ saam Wina'am la sa yɛlim ye, ‘Gu'usim ka da pian' bɛ'ɛd bɛɛ sʋm tis Jakob la.’

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: Ya'a an sida, m mi' ye fʋ bɔɔd nɛ fʋ sʋʋnr wʋsa ye fʋ kuli fʋ saam yir. Ka bɔzug kimm ka fʋ zuu m wina?”

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Ka Jakob lɛbis Laban ye, “Dabiem zug ka m zɔ, bɔzugɔ m tɛn'ɛs ye fʋ na faaɛn fʋ pu'ayuos la nɛ paŋ.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Amaa fʋ ya'a nyɛ ka sɔ' mɔri fʋ wina la, o sɔb na kpi! Vɛɛnsimi ti, ti du'adib la wʋsa nifin ka gɔs ye fʋ si'el bɛɛ m san'anɛɛ. Fʋ ya'a nyɛ si'el fʋn nɔkim.” Jakob da nam pʋ baŋ ye Rakɛl zu wina la.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Ka Laban kpɛn' Jakob dɔɔgin la, nɛ Lea dɔɔgin, nɛ ba tʋmtʋm pu'ab dɔɔdin la ka pʋ nyɛɛ. Ka o gaadi kpɛn' Rakɛl dɔɔgin la mɛn.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Li saŋa ka Rakɛl pʋn nɔk wina la niŋ o yʋgʋm ga'arin ka zin' li zug, ka Laban iee zin'ig wʋsa dɔɔg la pʋʋgin ka pʋ nyɛ si'ela.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Ka Rakɛl yɛl o saam ye, “M ba', da kɛ ka fʋ sʋʋnr duoe man zin' fʋ tuon la zugɔ, ka m kʋ nyaŋi duoe bɔzugɔ m paami m pier.” Ka Laban iee-iee dɔɔgin la wʋsa ka pʋ nyɛ wina la.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Ka Jakob sʋʋnr pɛlig ka o tans Laban ka bu'os o ye, “Taal bɔ kimm ka m tʋm? Bɛɛ tʋʋmbɛ'ɛd bɔ ka m tʋm ka fʋ kad mam paae kpɛla na?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Nannanna fʋn gɔsi m la'adin la wʋsa la, fʋ lauk bɔ ka fʋ nyɛ? Fʋ ya'a nyɛ lauk si'a, fʋn mɔri li na digili fʋ nidib nɛ mam nidib la tuon ka ba tisi ti babayi' la onɛ mɔr bʋʋd!

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: M nam mɔr ni'imi kɛɛn fʋ san'an na ye kɔnbid la yinne ka bʋnkɔnbbɛ'og gban'e. Hali baa li ya'a niŋ ala, m gban'adnɛ m mɛŋ pɛ'ogʋ yɔɔ li sam. Ka ba ya'a zu kɔnbʋg yʋ'ʋŋ bɛɛ nintaŋ fʋ yɛt ye m yɔɔmi li sam.

ref: I sweated every day, and I couldn't sleep at night because of the cold.
text: M bɛllim da an si'em la, ka nintaŋ nwɛ'ɛdim ka waad kʋʋdim ka gbɛɛm yii m nini ni.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: M yaanam Wina'am onɛ an Abraham Wina'am nɛ Isaak Wina'am ya'a ka'anɛ m san'anɛ, bi'esʋg kae ye fʋ naan kadin mam ka m keŋ nɛ nu'uzaalisɛ. Amaa Zugsɔb la nyɛ m namisʋg la zug, nɛ man tʋm nimmua siem la, din ka o sa zɛrigif yʋ'ʋŋin man zug la.”

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Laban lɛbis o ye, “Pu'ab la anɛ mam pu'ayuos, ba biis la mɛ anɛ man din, ka bʋnkɔnbid la anɛ man din mɛn. Fʋn nyɛt si'el la wʋsa anɛ mam bʋn. La'an nɛ wala, m na lɛn nyaŋi niŋ bɔ nɛ m pu'ayuos nwa' nɛ ba biis banɛ ka ba du'a la?

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: Kɛl ka mam nɛ fʋ maal nɔnaar, ka li an kasɛt man nɛ fʋ teŋsʋk.”

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Ka Jakob nɔk kugiri ba'a zi'el sappi ye li an kasɛt kugir,

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: ka yɛl o nidib la ye, “La'asimi kuga na!” Ka ba la'as kuga dʋ'ʋs dʋʋr. Ka ba wʋsa la'as taaba kuga la baba di diib.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Laban da pʋd o li yʋ'ʋr ye Jegar Sahaduta, ka Jakob mɛ buoli li o mɛŋ pian'adin ye Galeed.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Laban da yɛl ye, “Dʋʋr kaŋa anɛ kasɛt mam nɛ fʋn sʋʋgin.” Linzug ka ba da buon zin'ig la ye Galeed.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Li yʋ'ʋr yinne mɛ an Misipa, bɔzugɔ o yɛl ye, “Zugsɔb la na gɔs man nɛ fʋ ti wɛligir nwa' pʋʋgin.”

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: Laban da lɛn yɛl ye, “Fʋ ya'a pʋ maali m biis la sʋ'ʋŋa, bɛɛ fʋ tɔɔmnɛ di pu'a sieba, la'an nɛ man ka' anina la, baŋim ye Wina'am anɛ kasɛt man nɛ fʋ sʋʋgin.”

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Kuga dʋʋr la nɛ tiɛnr kugir nwa' anɛ kasɛti pa'an ye mam kʋ lɔ'ɔŋi kɛɛn fʋ san'an na daami fɔ, ka fʋ mɛ kʋ lɔ'ɔŋi li kɛn mam san'an na daami ma.

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Onɛ an Abraham Wina'am nɛ Nahor Wina'am, nɛ ba yaanam Wina'am la, na pa'al onɛ mɔr bʋʋd man nɛ fʋn sʋʋgin.” Ka Jakob mɛ pɔɔ o saam Isaak yʋ'ʋr zug ka siak nɔzi'elʋg la.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: O da maal maan zuoya teŋin la ka buol o nidib ka ba di. Ba da dii naae ka gbɛɛn anina.

ref: Early the next morning, Laban kissed his daughters and his grandchildren goodbye, then he left to go back home.
text: Ka di yim bɛogʋn asuba ka Laban kuak o yaas nɛ o pu'ayuos la pʋ'ʋsi ba sʋ'ʋŋa ka kaanb, kaanb sʋŋʋ tisi ba ka lɛbi kul.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Ka Jakob yii ani ken ka Wina'am maliaknam sia'al o.
